title: Lighting sources in the United Kingdom
subtitle: The share of lighting across the United Kingdom, sourced from different energy sources.
caption:
Lighting sources in the United Kingdom, 1700 to 2025
The share of lighting across the United Kingdom, sourced from different energy sources.

This stacked area chart tracks the proportional contribution of five distinct energy sources to total lighting in the United Kingdom over a period spanning more than three centuries, from 1700 to 2025. The vertical axis measures the share of lighting from 0% to 100%, while the horizontal axis marks time in 50-year increments. For the first 150 years of the timeline, candles dominate the energy mix, occupying nearly the entire vertical space and accounting for close to 100% of lighting through the mid-18th century. A transition begins in the early 19th century as whale oil enters the mix, visible as a grey band that expands rapidly between 1800 and 1850 to capture roughly 40% of the share. Kerosene appears briefly as a red layer in the late 19th century, peaking around 1880 before contracting. Gas lighting emerges as a major competitor in the latter half of the 19th century, represented by a purple area that grows to cover nearly half of the total share by 1900. The most significant structural shift occurs in the 20th century with the arrival of electricity. Starting from a negligible share around 1900, the blue area representing electricity expands steeply, surpassing gas by 1950 and reaching 100% by the early 21st century. By 2025, the chart shows a single-color field where electricity accounts for the entire share of lighting, while candles, whale oil, kerosene, and gas have all declined to zero. The data visualizes a complete turnover of energy infrastructure, moving from a single-source system to a diversified mix and finally to a new single-source dominance.
